speaker: interviewee
text: [BEEP]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is August 21, 1995. The interview with survivor Sulia Rubin, formerly Sulia Wolozhinski.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wolozhinski.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the interviewer is Elke Apelbaum Savoy. We are in Saddle River, New Jersey, USA. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Elke Apelbaum Savoy. I'm conducting an interview with Sulia Rubin. The interview is being conducted in Saddle River, New Jersey, in the United States of America. And today's date is August 21, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me your name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Sulia Rubin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-U-L-I-A. R-U-B-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's actually a Hebrew name. It's Shulamit. In Polish, it's Sulamit. And Sulia, is in English, because all of you pronounce the hard L. And I'm not [? Sola, ?] I'm [? Sula, ?] so I put the I in. And my maiden name is Wolozhinky. W-O-L-O-Z-H-I-N-K-Y, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't think so. Oh, yes, in school they called me Carioca. [LAUGHS] Because I used to dance Carioca a long time ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: May the 24th, 1924-- May the 22nd, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age now?

speaker: interviewee
text: 71, A big number.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell me the city and country of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Nowogrodek, Poland. This town is approximately 175 miles east-- kilometers east of the Russian border. And this town is a historical town and changed hands approximately every 20 years. So hence, when my mother and father were born in the same town, it was Russia. I was born, it was Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Today, it's [? Belarus, ?] White Russia, since the revolution in Russia. But it used to belong to the USSR.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the Jewish population in Nowogrodek?

speaker: interviewee
text: The population of the whole town, it wasn't big. It was small, about approximately 15,000-- 7,000 Jews. My childhood, I left Nowogrodek when I was approximately six weeks old. My parents went to Wilno and opened their practice there. And I guess I was the least likely person to survive, because I come from so-called intelligentsia. I didn't know any roads except when I went to camp or to a school for girls. And I was pretty assimilized, I'm ashamed to say. And I was a privileged child and a spoiled child, I guess. But even our spoiled children aren't like the spoiled children nowadays. Because you kind of were in awe of your parents. And I didn't realize how unusual they were. My mother was a fighter and a suffragette for freedom of women. And she was against the czar and against the pogroms. I'm the first generation grandmother to no grandchildren. I have never known mine. They were killed by the Cossacks. My mom was a dentist. My father comes from a long line of rabbis. Became quite nationalistic and atheistic. He became a Zionist, an ardent, ardent Zionist. He started as a rabbi, as a teacher, as a physician, laryngologist, and ended up as a periodontist to help my mother. My sister never knew my father. She was born while he was in Kiev, going to school. By the time he came back, she was four years old. And I was his daughter. He delivered me. So I had a, a very strong attachment to my father. I always respected and adored my mother. But by the time I became to understand her, it was too late. I almost lost her. And then it was gone. It was a unusual relationship between my sister and myself, too. She was five years older. And yet, we were like Siamese twins. We adored each other. When I was nine, I realized that I could never match her beauty. Because she won every beauty contest under the sun. And I decided that I might as well use my brain. So I decided I'll be the smart ones-- smart one. And anyway, it was easy at that time to be a smart. One because anybody who wasn't so pretty was smart. And the one who was pretty was usually considered, she isn't so smart. But my sister belied all of that norm. She was clever. She was good in school. She played the piano. She sang. We both were trained in ballet, which helped me later on during the war, because I entertained all the peasants. We played the piano. My mother played the piano and sang. And my father played the concertina. We were never punished, as I think back. We were never yelled at, as I think back. I never doubted that I was loved, although my mother worked. But looking back at it, I realized that I envied my girlfriends who had a mother constantly and mine was busy. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I miss them dearly. I find it hard to pray to God, but I speak to my parents daily on my walks. I speak to my sister. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Their birthdays are a torment. And no matter how old one gets and how much you have in life-- and I have my rewards-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you never can forget what was. It haunts you. You might call it-- what do you call it in English-- the Holocaust Syndrome. I suffer from it, too. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I dream nightly, or if not nightly I am a habitual insomniac. Because I have a dream that I'm running with both of my children. And you must know that one of my cousins perished like that. And on my right hand side is my older daughter, Anita, and my left hand side is my little one, five years younger, the same difference as between my sister and I, Ozelle. And behind us, I smell the Nazi. They smelled with tobacco. I can smell any soldier. I know their smells. And I hear his boots behind me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And lo and behold, I come to a big trench. And it's full of bodies. And he says, OK, you can live. But you have to choose one child, with which one? And I can never decide whether to jump inside or choose one. And I wake up in a sweat. And that's why I don't sleep. So I mean, it never, never leaves you. There is another-- it even when I drive the car, when I pass a tunnel, I say, oh my god, it would make a good shelter. But then I am afraid that if the bomb will fall, it will fill with water and I'll drown. I mean it never leaves you.

speaker: interviewee
text: When you go with a, with a bridge, you cross a bridge, they can cut us off anytime if they bomb the bridge. Thank God I live in New Jersey. It's open country. I can go to the rest of the [INAUDIBLE]. Because how when [INAUDIBLE]. They are only attached in one place, in the Bronx, to the rest of the land. I mean it never, never leaves you. I had a hard time fighting hatred. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I found out that I do not hate the young Germans. I cannot blame them for their generation-- what their parents did. I cannot hate the Poles. I cannot hate the Russians. I cannot hate the Lithuanians who killed us. And I cannot hate the Latvians and the Ukrainians. Hate is destructive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the only thing that I try to preserve is memory, memories. And basically, I can proudly say that my children are well-adjusted. They function. And they do not hate. They're forgiving people. Sulia, I'd like to know more about your childhood in Wilno and--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a ardent speaker. He spoke on [INAUDIBLE] Street, usually for the Zionistic movement. He was a very absent-minded man and a very good-looking man. I gather women found him attractive, but he had eyes only for my mother. And one time, he took me with him. I remember. And I was as absent-minded as he was. And he forgot me in the trolley car. And I forgot about him. I was about four. Suddenly, I woke up. And I didn't have Papa near me. So I walked over to the conductor. And I said, I would like to go to-- I was four, but I was a precocious child. By the time I was three and one half, I read in two languages. I taught myself. So when they brought me in, the policemen brought in, my father was speaking. By the way, he said, my wife is going to divorce me because I forgot my daughter in the trolley car. He was colorblind. Whenever he went to buy a hat, if he was told to buy a brown, he came with a green. He was a [POLISH], it's called in Polish. I don't know, probably in English, the same way. My mother was a petite woman. I don't know if she reached five feet. By American standards, she was pretty. Because she had high cheekbones and huge almond eyes and a pug nose and a wide mouth. And she was busty. But in those times, when a woman was in style with the rosebud mouth and no cheekbones, and flat-chested, she wasn't considered pretty. But she used to get compliments that she is as clever as a man. And if the lawyers in the morning didn't come to ask her advice, they lost the case. She was brilliant and fiery. When she walked in in the room, you know that she was there. Papa was quiet. There was no machoism in our house, because my father was a very secure man. He didn't mind at all that he-- I think he would be very unhappy with a submissive wife. He like her independence. The only the time I saw my father cry is when my mother went-- she never cut her hair. Her hair was long. And she had braids. When she let them down, they reached the, the heels. And at that time, just like we had here, Vidal Sasoon, at that time was in style La Garconne. And she went and cut off her braids.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she came back. And Papa said to her, what did you do with your hair? And she said, well, between my bust and my hair, I was always afraid I'll fall in into a patient's mouth. So I cut it off.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he cried. This is the first time I saw my father cry. But the braids were with us for the longest time, because my sister and I used to play with them. It was a happy childhood, happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My mother rebelled because my grandfather was an extremely religious man. And my grandma was from a small town. And like there was a matchmaker. She was a pretty girl. And she married my grandfather. She had with him six children. But there was no love in the sense of American way. And she helped her daughters to run through the window to avoid her, her faith. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother adored her grandmother-- her mother, my grandmother. My grandfather, she said he was limited because he was so religious. My father rebelled altogether. We have on the Lower East Side here, the [INAUDIBLE] [? Wolosian ?] rabbis. I'm a descendant from them because I'm a [? Kahane ?] from the tribe that came from there. But they don't recognize me. To them, I am dead, because I'm not orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the background was unusual for those times. And I feel fortunate that I knew them, though a short time, but that I am part of my parents. In genetics, I have a lot of my mother in me and a lot of my father. And I hope it went on to our daughters. How did other people view your parents? Were they unusual for their time-- the neighbors, people in town?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had her friends from college. In Wilno, she was in her environment. When she came back to Nowogrodek-- we came in 19-- my mother came in '36. I finished my-- I was in a boarding school. I finished my school year. And then I came-- in 1937, I started Nowogrodek. Now that I look back at Nowogrodek, I love it. But at those times, I didn't. Because you didn't have any sewage. The Jews were very clannish and very religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: I had a dog. What do you do with a dog on Yom Kippur? You walk the dog on a, on a leash. Not in Nowogrodek. Now, I didn't know that I wasn't allowed to eat ham. We ate it. So I went and bought it openly. Wasn't so. People when they did it, they did it quietly.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't, I wasn't accustomed-- we observed holidays. We knew who we were. We were proud of being Jewish. But we were not very religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did people do when you bought ham? How did they react?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was insulted by the kids. I was called names. I was caught in the middle. I didn't belong to the Poles. And I did not exactly belong to, to my friends. I was an outsider. And every vacation, I went to my friends to Wilno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did your family move to Nowogrodek?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister fell in love with a lefty, with a revolutionary. And he gave her some illegal papers, illegal newspapers, to put it in the dressing room or in the check room of the teachers. And she was caught. And she was arrested. And we were on the blacklist. And my parents started losing patients. And rocks we were-- rocks were thrown into our windows with all kinds of-- so my mother knew a Polish general. Kowalski was his name. And he gave her a contract, which he did before. Every summer, she worked for the, for the Polish Army. And we children used to have-- what do you call-- an orderly. We came with our [POLISH] with our nanny. And we had quite a good time with them. And we used to stay on the Latvian border in a town, Druya. And from there, my uncle wrote to my mother that if you have to sit already in a, in a small hole of a village, you might as well come home. So they went before. And I was left in a convent.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing in a convent?

speaker: interviewee
text: The school was a Catholic school, but it was coeducational. And I was very fond of the, of the sisters. And they were fond of me. Religion wasn't forced upon me, Catholic religion. But I think I'm a good housekeeper up till today, thanks to them. I really mean it. You had to make a bed, that if you put 50 groszy-- it's like a quarter or whatever-- and it used to jump up. I was wakened at 4:00. I'm disciplined, thanks to, thanks to them. I think that, thanks to their discipline, I survived a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at the time you were with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: 12. And you know what? I used to come sometimes and stand in church. And I wished I weren't Jewish. It was so much easier not to be Jewish. Because at that time, one of the professors, a woman, got married. And she invited everybody, the whole class, to the ceremony in church, except children from other faith, number one, me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was actually the second time in my life I stood up for my Jewishness. The first one was when my mother was on the border when the general got her the, the contract. And he had two sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a Jewish peddler was coming on the road, you know, with pots and pans, whatever. And he was screaming in a-- like I speak in accented English, his Polish was no better. So he was, pots, pans to sell. And they started throwing rocks at, at him. And I said, why are you throwing rocks at him? He said, he's a zhid. He's a kike.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, but I'm Jewish, too. Well, you are a different Jew. I said, oh, no. I am not a different Jew. Maybe I speak Polish. I am dressed, but he's my brother. I never played with them again. I was six years old. That was the first one. The second one, I stood up, so it shows you a character of a child that I have guts even then.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the professor didn't invite me to the wedding. And when she came back and everybody congratulated her, she called my name out [? Woloriska. ?] I stood up. She says, no congratulations from me-- from you to me? I said, no. Officially, I don't know even that you got married. I wasn't invited. She says, what do you mean? You are not going to congratulate me? I said, no. She sent me to the principal. I refused. I don't know what transpired there, but she wasn't teaching there more.

speaker: interviewee
text: The principal, who loved me-- I was a good student-- got rid of her somehow. I was often-- when my parents left-- I was often change-- chased by my Polish friends. I was good in class to help them in their Polish. It's like a janitor who speaks French, but doesn't know how to write well or spell. The same thing was with the Poles. I knew Polish from the [? Rutsan ?] because I had to. They didn't. They spoke it. That was their language, so I used to help them. And I was sitting on one bench with the Princess [INAUDIBLE]. She was a relative of the [INAUDIBLE]. My mother always pushed me in very fancy schools. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I helped her. And she never defended me. When I went out of school, they chased me. And I still remember the taste of horse manure where my face was pushed in. And they called me that I'm the killer of Christ. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when I said that Christ was Jewish, I was beaten up more. I learned not to complain. I never told my parents. I never snitched at my friends. Until they found another Jew to torment, they left me alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these are incidents that I blamed my mother later on. I used to say, you didn't you give me the religiousness and the belonging with my people. Because you are not religious, I am not. Tradition, I was taught. Religion, I was taught but I didn't practice. And I am, I am called a Pole of Jewish faith. But I am not a Pole. What am I?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was confusing for a child. But I knew one thing, that it gave me a backbone. It taught me. But not one stuck up for me, all of those Polish friends. So I avoided them. I said to myself, I am better than you. I am a better student. I understand better. And I will grow up to be something and you'll be nothing. And that's how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is when you were 12?

speaker: interviewee
text: 12. When I was nine, I made up my mind I cannot fall in love with every guy my sister goes out with. Because I can't match her beauty. And at 12, I decided that I am my own person.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then after you were 12?

speaker: interviewee
text: After I was 12, I came to Nowogrodek. I was 13. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I trusted everybody. I still love all of my friends. I couldn't say a bad word about them if I was tormented. All of them were good. Were-- was everybody nice to me? No. I was popular with the boys. It's not because I was so gorgeous. Even a dog would be popular. There was a majority of boys and very, very few girls. [LAUGHS] So I can't take it upon me. But I was lively. And I was the president of the class. And I was the president of the-- and the editor of the newspaper. And I danced. And I sang. I was in the show. I was on the stage. I was a doer. And I was lively, I guess, and a talker. And envy is a normal thing in people. And I realized it, that I cannot blame my peers for being envious. I, fortunately enough, have never been eaten by envy or jealousy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I have learned another thing. My mother used to say to me, when you marry a guy, you must marry a guy who love you more than you love him. So you will never be hurt. Couldn't understand it. When I was 14, I had an infatuation with a 17-year-old older man. He was, [LAUGHS] he was in high school. And he was a very attractive boy. And he had a date with me. He was supposed to take me to the movies. And he came to the house. And I had one of my best friends-- he was a shy boy-- she came to do the, to the stoop. And I don't know what she said to him, but I saw through the window. Don't you know, he left me and he went with her to the movies. That was the first time in my life a boy stood me up and never again. When he came again, I gave him the letters she wrote to me to camp. And I said, from now on, we are good friends. But I'm not going with you steady, because there are many boys. And I don't need you. I cried a long time. And when I saw him, it hurt, But never did a guy leave me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because of this guy, I belonged to a Zionist organization. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh really?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was forbidden by the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zionist organizations were forbidden by the Polish people, not while you went to gymnasium-- to their schools. When you were out of the school, you could do it. They considered it too rebellious and too political. But that's where I learned about Palestine, about Israel. And I fell deeply in love with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you become political?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in a way, yes, to the, to the dismay of my mother. [LAUGHS]
